I was offered a Care Credit card. It also had a Mastercard logo. The providers I had accepted that card.  I
activated it within 30 days and had confirmation that it was activated.
I went to a dental clinic and tried to use it, and it was declined.  I called and they said my card had been
cancelled because I did not activate it.  They activated my account and sent a new card.
The new card they sent did not have the Mastercard logo.  I tried to use it at a local clinic and they did not
take it because it didn't have the MC logo. I checked and there are very few providers that accept this
card without the logo.
I feel like the victim of a bait and switch. Get issued the good card that I could have used everywhere and
instead get a card only the dental office takes. I have dentures and go there once every few years. This
card is essentially useless to me for anything except for very few places I do not have a need for.
Jo Clark

Together, we do more

A funding solution that supports
your business and your customers.
WELCOME TO UNITED CREDIT
Welcome! We’re excited you’re part of our merchant network.
United Credit is proud to be your partner and support your business growth through a ﬁnancing option that helps
consumers get the services and products they need.
We have relationships with multiple lenders, which means we have a variety of ﬁnancing options to serve a wide variety of
your customers. Our team is here to support you and your business every step of the way so you can focus on what
matters most: your customers.
Ready to get started? This guide will help you through the process.
01

How we help
YOUR BUSINESS
YOUR CUSTOMERS
Better options
We have relationships with multiple lenders, which
means just one application gives customers access to
various ﬁnancing options.
Simple terms
Customers have access to competitive interest with
terms from 6 months up to 60 months.
Quick pre-approval
Customers can apply at the point of sale using our
online form and receive a decision in minutes.
Dedicated support
Our easily-accessible and fast  application - as well as
our customer support team - guides your customers
through the process, taking the burden oﬀ your staﬀ.
No penalties
Loans offered by our lending partners through the
UC platform have no prepayment penalties.
02
Bigger loans
We can provide loans up to $25,000 to help
customers pay for larger expenses.
Strong approvals
We support your ongoing growth with strong
consumer approval rates*.
Quick funding
Once a customer is approved and secures ﬁnancing,
your business receives payment quickly.
Ongoing support
We offer live customer service and account
managers to support your business.
No hidden fees
We never charge enrollment or setup fees. There are
no monthly or annual fees, either - you only pay fees
when a loan is processed.
*United Credit partners with multiple lenders, some of whom offer
2nd look options for consumers with sub-prime credit, which
expands opportunities for approvals. Ask us for more details.

The United Credit advantage
When it comes to ﬁnancing a major or larger purchase, your customers need options.
Here’s how United Credit can help, and how we compare to other ﬁnancing options.
We are not a credit card.
United Credit provides your customer with access to an installment loan, not a credit card. That means we help them get
ﬁnancing for exactly what they need, nothing more. We break those payments into easy-to-understand terms, a set APR,
and monthly payments that are easy to manage.
We can be a stronger option than personal checks or cash.
Some customers may have savings they’d like to use for their purchases. While this is an option, it could mean draining their
personal savings or emergency fund to cover the cost. Using a ﬁnancing option for their purchase keeps that cash on hand
in case it’s needed later. Plus, they can use it to pay oﬀ their loan early without a penalty!
03

Oﬀering ﬁnancing to customers
Want to talk to your customers about their ﬁnancing options but aren’t sure where
to begin? Here are some tips to get the conversation started.
Have a conversation
Tell your customers financing is offered through lending
partners on the United Credit platform. If they have
questions, refer them to our website at unitedcredit.com.
Give them time
Some customers may be ready to make a purchase right
away, and others may need time to consider their options.
Do not pressure them into financing. Oﬀer them patience
and kindness along the way, ensuring they know you want
them to make the best decision for themselves and their
budget.
Share information
Let every customer know your business offers financing.
Even if they aren’t interested in ﬁnancing their purchase in
that moment, they may consider it later when they are
ready to make that major purchase.

attachment_2: MERCHANT TRANSPARENCY GUIDELINES
United Credit promotes full transparency and disclosure to all applicants. To assure that applicants are not
mislead and are aware of key information about United Credit, you hereby agree as follows:
● United Credit works with multiple lenders to offer closed end, unsecured, personal
installment loans to borrowers.
● No person on your staff who has not completed training for the United Credit program will discuss
the program with prospective borrowers.
● You and your staff will not make any implied or overt promises that an applicant will be approved
for a loan through United Credit
● You and your staff will not quote any particular interest rate, term, or other loan feature to a
prospective borrower with United Credit
● You and your staff agree you will print adverse action notices at the request of the prospective
borrower.
● You will direct all potential applicants to apply directly through the United Credit application by
providing them an application link via the merchant portal OR with a United Credit brochure that
includes our website application link.
● You will not suggest/direct patients/clients who are under duress (general sedation, in extreme
discomfort, or heavily medicated) to apply through United Credit
● You will not suggest/direct patients/clients to apply with United Credit if you have reason to believe
they are intending to commit fraud.
● You will direct all patient/client inquiries about the program or their loan to United Credit for
handling or referral to the appropriate lender as applicable.
● You will accept United Credit loan proceeds only for those costs incurred or services rendered within
30 days of receipt of funds. If services are not rendered within 30 days, the borrower has the right to
an automatic refund from you for services not yet rendered.
● You will maintain a fair refund policy and will advise all patients/clients of your policy.
● Comply with all United Credit policies and procedures and all applicable local, state and federal laws
and regulations, including non-discrimination laws and the customer’s right of rescission
● United Credit reserves the right to monitor your adherence to these and other United Credit policies.
Providers who violate these policies will be subject to chargebacks as well as potential termination
from the program

attachment_4: Date:  7/10/2023

To:
Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services
(CMS), Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), Department of the Treasury
(Treasury)
Cc:
Federal Reserve, X9 ISO20022 Working Group on Faster Healthcare Payments
From:  Kendra Wyatt, New Birth Company LLC, Chief Executive Officer
Re:
Request for Information Regarding Medical Payment Products

I am answering this RFI as a State Licensed Healthcare Provider, who is in-network with Kansas
and Missouri Medicaid, Tricare, Federal Exchange Insurance Products and 3rd Party Commercial
Insurance Plans.  I am reviewing the use of medical payment credit cards for my business right
now due to high deductible maternity benefits and slow insurance plan payments.

The bluntness in my RFI response reflects the intolerable length of time it takes for small
healthcare businesses to be paid by Insurance Plans and TPAs reviewing claims. 60+ days is
common for Federal Exchange and TPA plans. In some cases, we are faxing and mailing claims.
These systems can’t support the 10s of ICD10 codes needed to back up modern Social
Determinant of Health (SODH) risk assessments.

We can’t talk about faster payments in healthcare without talking about the impact of
ISO20022 on the American banking system and that impact on the small business owner. Large
banks and vendors are working on ISO20022 adoption and the ability to bill their customers
(me) in real time.  Large institutions will leverage fast-payment bank products into basis point
discounts and small business providers will be stuck with no banking float.

Mr. Mick Talley and I co-chair an X9 ISO20022 Faster Healthcare Payments Working Group. I
recommend you interview me, Mr. Talley, Mr. Stephen Ranzini with University Bank and Mr.
David Repking at JPMorganChase, Chair of X9C and ISO 20022 Market Practice Industry Forum.
Guy Berg and Carolyn Kroll with the Chicago Federal Reserve are key leaders in the faster
payments space.  I recommend talking to Professor Ge Bai, John Hopkins regarding Hospitals
non-for-profit tax status.

In the spirit of “if you oppose, propose” I ask the Biden Administration, CFPB, CMS, HHS, the
ONC, Treasury and the Federal Reserve to sponsor 4 Corner Pilots of “Faster Payments” in the
healthcare sector.  We already have a (4 Corner) healthcare use case using the Federal Reserve
framework. Let’s learn by doing. We are underutilizing our national HIT infrastructure; and
driving out small business providers in our underserved, rural and tribal communities. Doing
nothing represents the status quo of friction in 18.3% of the GDP. The use of medical credit
cards is a symptom; the disease is friction and lack of innovation.  We must create the low-
friction policy environment where healthcare providers can survive and thrive.  Let’s support
alternate market forces to compete head-to-head with robber barons who make money off the
friction.  Thank you for listening.  I welcome your inquiries regarding my responses.



What are the benefits, costs, and risks of medical payment products for consumers, health
care providers, and companies offering these products?
Our APRN Midwife Led & State Licensed Birth Center provides low-risk pregnant women with
prenatal, birth, and birth facility services for mothers and their newborns.  We represent the
best-case scenario of a low-risk birth and appropriate resourced birth experience.

There is a myth in US healthcare that “Pregnancy & Birth” is covered 100% by all American
insurance plans.  This is simply NOT THE CASE.  High deductibles are being applied to low-risk
birth.

Plan deductibles of $2000, $3000, $4000, and $5000 are commonly applied deductibles for
maternity services.  HHS needs to compare the differences in payments made through
Medicaid, Federal Employee Health Plan and the Federal Exchange Plans.  The Federal Exchange
Plans have the least programming and the highest deductibles for Maternity Services. The cost
of the newborn is paid separately from the mother, adding additional cost.  I have no desire to
take these young families to small claims court, yet my unpaid patient accounts receivables is
currently over $100,000.

What are the terms of medical payment products, including interest rates and fees?
My understanding it varies based on the terms of the loan. I’ve attached marketing material
from one company.

How much debt do consumers carry on medical credit cards and loans in total, and what is
the average individual debt level?  If we move forward with this product, the patient debt will
be from $1000-$5000 dollars reflecting their plan deductible.

How concentrated is the medical payment product market, and what role do private equity
firms play in this market?  I do not know.  I only have exposure to Care Credit and United
Credit.

Are there specific populations (e.g., race, socioeconomic status, gender identity, sexual
orientation, age, language, etc.) or geographic regions that experience disproportionately
higher utilization of medical payment products?  Look at Maternity Care. Look at States where
there is no Medicaid expansion and where the Medicaid income levels for Maternity coverage
are low.

What are the health equity impacts of medical payment products and related billing and
collection policies and practices? Many OB, Pediatric, Dentist and Orthodontic Offices do not
participate in Medicaid.  So, by definition, access to medical credit is only way for these patients
to access highly desirable services where the Provider refuses to accept less than
Medicare/Cost based reimbursement.


It is an American policy failure that we haven’t adopted aligned multi-payer policy and payment
environment for high utilization and highly desirable outcomes.
Align Birth across Medicaid, Federal Exchange, Tricare, VA CCN, Federal Employee Health Plan.
PROTIP:  **Providers will change behavior if you pay them quickly. **

Kansas Medicaid pays the Pregnancy and Birth Global of 59400 70% of Medicare.  Certified
Nurse Midwives are only paid 75% of the OB Global, which is only 50% of Medicare.  The Birth
Center reimbursement of $1295 hasn’t been increased for 20 years.

Do medical payment products affect members of specific underserved communities
differently, including members of Tribal communities and geographically isolated
communities?  I’m thinking many of these places of no access to capital.  Ask Matt Libman, CEO
of United Credit and Beto Casellas, CEO of Care Credit where they offer their product.

Do certain products or policies present opportunities to better serve members of
underserved communities? Yes. Suggestion….US Government sponsors pregnant families with
personal health bank accounts with first $10,000 to purchase maternity/birth care as they see
fit, subject to IRS eligibility criteria, allowing families to bank unused amount in child’s name.
Implement this for all Americans including Medicaid eligible. Any amount over $10,000 subject
to healthcare insurance rules and case management.

Patients can pay for care in many different ways, such as by medical credit card or loan,
general purpose credit card, insurance, or through a zero-interest payment plan. What are
the costs and benefits for health care providers of offering each of these methods? Are there
situations where one method of payment is more advantageous than another?
As a small provider, I have to accept credit cards for these deductables or I wouldn’t be paid.  I
lose 5% to the cost of credit card processing. I have no ability to finance long term payment
plans. Large provider health systems have plenty of capital to support this as an option.  Thus,
our current economic system empowers very large systems over small entrepreneurs and
business owners.
What incentives do financial services companies offer health care providers, including
revenue-sharing or other financial or non-financial incentives? Largest incentive to take this
product is time.   I’m having to pay a fee upfront to recoup 85% of the fee that would typically
take me months to receive; and that is after I bug and nag and send people to collections.  We
are failing people.

The rich have the most free time, the rich never wait in line in person, don’t have to fax paper,
don’t have to “go down to the agency”.  HCA or Kaiser can chase down $1000 when it has
rooms of people to wait people out and lawyers to take people to small claims court.  See
attached list of Non for Profit Hospital Cases at Small Claims Court.



How do medical payment products and health insurance coverage interact? Do group health
plan or health insurance issuer practices contribute to uptake of medical payment products
by patients and providers?  The medical payment product market is a direct result of slow
payments by insurance companies and pathetic plan design for high utilization procedures such
as birth. Instead of innovating more efficient care delivery models and faster payments to
incent quality; your plans are moving costs to higher deductibles.

How many days do providers typically have to wait to be paid by plans or issuers versus by
medical payment product companies or general purpose credit card companies? My
credit/debit processor settles within 48 hours.  The fastest insurance plan can pay a claim in
two weeks for a perfect claim.  The TPAs and Federal Exchange Plans can easily take up to 90
days and that is based on us chasing them down repeatedly.  The TPA plans have the highest
deductibles, thus the medical credit market was born.  ISO20022 predicts I’m paid in minutes.

What factors, such as administrative requirements or clinical reviews, contribute to any
differential resolution timelines?  Yes- because CMS requires fee for service claims as a
requirement of participation. These claims are rekeyed by hand by person and people make
many mistakes. We should be paid off of our clinical data in our EHRs.

Please direct HHS, CMS, Office of the National Coordinator and Federal Reserve to pilot 4
Corner payment models in 2023.  We’ve had the ONC and federally funded EHRs for 13 years.
Why aren’t Plans paying on clinical data instead of claims starting with the most simple of
pediatric and maternal/women’s health visits and birth?   You start at the beginning with the
highly desired, predictable easy transactions. The best security against fraudulent payments is
rapid payments incenting the provider to give up 100% transparent clinical data.

Does a patient’s use of a medical payment product exempt them from certain consumer
protections, provider requirements, or group health plan or health insurance issuer
requirements? No. Not that I’m aware of.

Are different types of health coverage treated differently?
Yes.  Let’s consider the history of racial segregation on American pregnancy and birth. We are in
a multi-payer Safety Net/FQHC/Academic Medical Center vs the Private Suburban and Rural
markets fragmented reality.    As a result, we don’t have the Adam Smith economic market
driving equity and outcomes.  We need the Federal Government to align agencies towards an
outcome. Not all consumers have equal access to all Providers.

Does health care provider organizational structure, including ownership by private equity,
affect providers’ decisions to offer and promote these products?
Sure.  Yes. Does this factor compare to taking or not taking low Medicaid payments ??  We
wouldn’t exist if I were a non-for-profit.  Funding my own business was the only way to raise
capital.  I take both states Medicaid.


What are some best practices for health care providers who offer medical payment products
in avoiding adverse financial and health impacts for patients? i. Are there specific tactics or
practices that are well tailored and adapted for use by health care professionals in and
serving underserved communities, including Tribal communities and geographically isolated
communities?
See great idea of funding first $10,000 for every pregnancy/child. Empower the people not the
institutions.

What actions should the agencies take to develop and encourage uptake of these established
best practices? iii. Are there examples of actions or best practices at the State or local level to
which the Federal government should look?
Google “Child to College” and “Baby Bonds”.  There are many people across the political
spectrum that would be likely to support the first $10,000 idea.  Invest in American family’s
financial literacy during pregnancy and birth.  Set them up for success.

To what extent are patients using medical payment products to pay bills that are incorrect, or
that could be covered or defrayed by lower-cost alternatives?
Better plan design for Birth; eg cover 100% and these products won’t have a market in
maternity care.  Start there.   There is so much infertility due to untreated diabetes.  Let’s get
healthcare for women right first.

What billing errors may patients commonly encounter?
Patient not eligible.  Not aware less than 26 years old are on their parent or step parent plan.
Baby not included on Parent/Grandparent Plan.  Plan deems Provider not eligible or not in-
network.  Need one single CAQH like system.  The idea that you must credential your six
providers with every plan and TPA is a terrible Provider burden and waste of everyone’s time.

How does using a medical payment product affect patients’ rights to dispute incorrect bills?
It shouldn’t.  That isn’t in our best interest.  Many providers game the system as an “out of
network” provider.  We don’t. Using the medical payment plan is last resort assuming Mom is in
good standing.  Again, we are talking about poor plan design with $4000 deductibles. New
parents know the least about navigating insurance and have the least time to figure it out.

Are certain groups of patients, such as members of specific underserved communities, more
likely to experience medical billing errors or issues resolving disputes over bills paid using
medical payment products? It is our experience that the most vulnerable working Americans,
whether in the urban core or in rural communities have the most poorly designed benefits.  If
they work for the school, they have high deductibles. If they work at a small business, they have
high deductibles.   (NOTE- look at Nashville Public School and Vanderbilt for example of doing it
right).  The inequity is that only the richest most affluent people working at corporate offices
have the best benefits.  The poorest have access to Medicaid but equal access to all Providers.
Ineligible Moms in labor are lucky if they are served when they walk into the ER under EMTALA.


What actions should agencies consider taking to better understand the effects of medical
payment products on consumers and the health care industry, educate consumers and
providers about the risks of these products, and collect complaints?
Send my RFI comments around.  You should start with fixing Birth.  This financial situation is
depressing the birth rate.  There is a bureaucratic fear of addressing pregnancy head on due to
abortion. Get healthy birth right. Increase likelihood good things can happen.

What are some sources of data on medical payment products? What additional data are
needed to understand the impact of medical payment products on patients and the health
care industry?  Backward engineer Federal Exchange Plan claims with high deductibles.
Analyze those CPTs. Ask direct questions. Ask the credit companies to give testimony.

What data collection, data analysis, and research actions should agencies take? iii. Are there
different or other actions that agencies should consider for underserved communities,
including Tribal communities and geographically isolated communities?
Identify and pay Community Health Workers in underserved, rural and Tribal communities to
ask around.  People close to the trenches know the answers.  Have you asked your FQHC
partners how people are making it work?

What types of consumer complaints have States and localities received?
No idea.  My complaint is that we OK every drug that comes on the market, but we can’t pay for
a healthy birth that would make the drug not necessary in the future.  Our values are screwed
up.

Where medical payment products are causing harm, what are some specific levers for
regulatory oversight and enforcement by Federal agencies that regulate financial products or
health care providers?
We are talking about outpatient procedures vs inpatient procedures right ?  These products are
sold as loans vs credit cards…is a bank involved?  Who holds the funds for the Credit
companies?  What regulatory oversight do you have over a clinic when the Plan isn’t Medicare?
You don’t have that oversight at the federal level.  You should be pushing best practice out
through the Federal Exchange Plans and Federal Employee Health Plan to impact BCBS, United
and Centene.  You must partner with States and Boards of Nursing and Boards of Healing Arts
to solicit and educate on complaints.  You can also check with malpractice companies.

Are there specific areas for Federal enforcement actions? Are there examples of regulation or
enforcement at the State or local level to which the Federal government should look?
Go fix plan design first.  Skipping to law enforcement when we haven’t fixed the main problem
is half our SDOH problems in this country. Focus on removing demand from the market.





What complementary legislative actions are worth exploring? Where may additional
statutory authority be needed?
Identify the difference between the necessary use of these products that reflect policy failure
and poor plan design (Birth) vs funding of Dog Hip Replacement (discretionary income).  And
then use that data to inform legislative action.

I have not used this type of product myself. I’m not going to comment on this section
regarding Individuals.

What actions should the CFPB consider taking to address problematic practices related to
medical credit cards or loans, including debt collection and credit reporting practices ?
Assess the reasons why the cards are being used the first place.  See above.

How do firms offering medical financial products typically market to providers?
We are cold-called/solicited through our licenses (public/purchasable), trade associations and
membership organizations.

How do creditors and their affiliates underwrite loans to patients? What specific factors (e.g.,
age, type of medical procedure, credit score, etc.) are considered in underwriting?
I don’t know.  They seem to be happy to support “Birth” given the large deductibles described.
Eg, “they have 75 providers in this space”.

Do consumers understand the risks of paying medical bills via a medical credit card,
installment loan, or other commercial payment product, including lowered ability to
negotiate their bill with their provider?
If their choices are being take to small claims court over their 20% co-pay and $3000 deductible
or using a card; they are making a rational choice to open an account and pay the bill.  Do you
see how close we are to debtor’s prisons? Garnishing wages for a healthcare bill?

To what extent are alleged debts placed on medical credit cards and loans sent to debt
collectors? How do medical payment product companies’ debt collection practices differ from
those of health care providers, and are any issuer or provider debt collection practices posing
risks to consumers?
I have no idea how they differ.  Being threatened with court is scary.  People calling you day and
night and calling your relatives is scary.  It puts you in a constant state of stress, depression, and
leads to cardiac conditions.  It contributes to substance abuse.  See “these people are the ones
who can least afford these large deductibles”.

How can the CFPB use its authorities to ensure people with medical bills in collections,
including medical payment product debt, are screened for eligibility for financial assistance
and other benefits?
You need a healthcare stabilization fund and availability fund as the “back up” for the indigent
not yet eligible for Medicare, Medicaid and Disability SSI and SSID.   Google “healthcare

stabilization fund” for examples of how states created funds as a last resort for provider
malpractice.

How are health care providers and financial companies using credit or “propensity to pay”
scores to determine patients’ eligibility for financial assistance or medical payment products?
I’m looking at their deductible.  If it’s over $1000 and there isn’t proof of payment plan/income,
then they would meet criteria to receive information on the product.

What are the implications for compliance with the Fair Credit Reporting Act or other CFPB
authorities?
No idea.

When hospitals write off a patient’s debt as uncollectible or “bad debt” and cease attempts
to collect, do they notify patients that collection attempts will cease?
I don’t know.  Ask Hospitals.  Ask Ambulatory Surgery Centers. We provide a final letter that the
person is in good standing if they make good faith attempts with us to pay their bill.  We are on
a cash basis. Look at IRS policy how to lift up small healthcare providers with annual revenue
less than 5 Million per year who are in-network with Medicaid.

Would patients benefit from such notifications, and would such notifications reduce hospital
revenue? See Ge Bai at John Hopkins. Jumbo non-for-profit hospitals suing its own patients.
Where’s the charity care?  https://www.healthaffairs.org/doi/abs/10.1377/hlthaff.2020.01627

What actions should HHS consider taking to address problematic practices related to medical
credit cards or loans, particularly as they relate to patients eligible for or enrolled in
Medicare, Medicaid, or the Children’s Health Insurance Program, or patients enrolled in
Affordable Care Act Marketplace plans?
Start with the ACA Marketplace high deductible plans and paying for pregnancy and birth.
This is a policy failure.

What types of health insurance (Medicare, Medicaid, private insurance, etc.) are particularly
associated with the likelihood that an individual is offered or makes use of medical credit
cards and loans, and how does the type of health coverage affect relevant provider billing
practices?
ACA Marketplace plans followed by Self Insured Employers with Third Party Administrators are
the WORST offenders creating the poor plan design circumstances where these products are
needed.  Overlay high-deductible plans with low poverty requirements (must be very poor) to
obtain Medicaid.  Here are your problem areas.

Are there particular health care provider types that are most associated with being offered or
offering medical payment products, and are these providers receiving directed payments or
other incentives through State Medicaid programs?
Certified Nurse Midwives and Birth Centers are not being solicited.


Has the No Surprises Act and its surprise billing protections affected the prevalence and use
of medical credit cards and loans, and if so, how? i. Has the notice and consent process been
used to promote medical cards and loans to patients seeking health care items or services
from out-of-network providers/facilities, and if so, how?
We are just seeing the beginning of transparency for birth services come through on the
Hospital Web Sites.  There is still much confusion as the Hospital isn’t clear on what is a Facility
Charge vs the Professional Charge, Anesthesia, Facility for Mom, Facility Charge for Baby,
Pediatrician bill.  Please come down on the Hospitals and make this crystal clear for a basic
NVSD and a normal newborn. These are the #1 utilized hospital admissions and we still don’t
have basic consumer transparency.  There are still many opportunities for “surprises”.

For example, in instances where the No Surprises Act permits providers and facilities to seek
notice and obtain consent from an insured patient to waive their balance billing and cost-
sharing protections under the No Surprises Act, are providers and facilities impermissibly
attaching or incorporating medical card or loan documents or information to the notice and
consent forms, or giving them to the patient at the same time as the notice and consent
forms?
I can’t speak to this. Talk to hospitals.

What steps are health care providers and facilities putting into place to ensure that bills paid
through medical payment products do not violate surprise billing requirements and that
patients who use medical payment products retain their No Surprises Act rights?
I can’t speak to this.  Talk to hospitals.
How does or might the use of medical credit cards and loans affect the amount and timing of
cost sharing a patient covered through Medicare, Medicaid, and/or the Affordable Care Act
Marketplace owes for a covered service?
It shouldn’t affect either.  The allowable should be the same.

Are there any observable differences in cost sharing among patients belonging to
underserved communities, such as Tribal communities or geographically isolated
communities?
Underserved and rural community members covered by ACA or Employer sponsored TPA plans
tend to have higher co-insurance percentages and higher deductibles.  It isn’t fair at all.

Hospital Price Transparency: What prices or versions of standard charges (e.g., cash prices)
are offered to patients who sign up for a medical credit card or installment loan?
I don’t know.  Ask the Hospitals.

What steps are taken by health care providers to ensure these charges are adequately
disclosed in accordance with hospital price transparency requirements? Do these charges
reflect and specifically identify facility fees?
We are just seeing the beginning of transparency for birth services come through on the
Hospital Web Sites.  There is still much confusion as the Hospital isn’t clear on what is a Facility
Charge vs the Professional Charge, Anesthesia, Facility for Mom, Facility Charge for Baby,

Pediatrician. Please come down on the Hospitals and make the total cost for a basic NVSD and a
normal newborn. These are the #1 utilized hospital admissions and we still don’t have basic
consumer transparency.

How might HHS improve patient understanding of options for covering the cost of medical
treatments? At what points in the care process could patients be provided with information
about their financial obligations and payment options?
We should have the ability to purchase a birth bundle and see true apples to apples on quality
with CNMs and Birth Centers. For the 1000th time, the family healthcare literacy journey starts
when a woman is pregnant and gives birth. It’s the first time we use our insurance. You educate
her and her partner on price transparency; you are educating her family for the lifespan.   She is
family medical case manager 80% of the time.  Dear Treasury and Federal Reserve- invest in
Mom/Partner Baby Financial Literacy as part of Pregnancy Childbirth Classes.  Let’s go pilot
these together and invest in American families.

What financial relationships exist between medical payment product companies and health
care providers? For example, do companies provide financial incentives to providers who
enroll patients in medical payment products?
None to my knowledge.  It’s a service sold for a fee.

Do providers pay financial companies to collect patients’ overdue balances? Or, do providers
have arrangements with financial companies to indemnify the company in whole or in part if
the patient defaults, such as an arrangement that when patients default on their debt to the
financial company, the debt reverts to the provider?
Yes, providers pay their billing companies to collect balances every day as part of the 5.75% we
are charged to process claims on top of the 5% we lose to credit card processing fees for paid
deductibles.  Some providers “sell” their debt to collection agencies.  Ask the big guys who do
that- look them up by NAIC codes.

Do health care providers or financial institutions market or recommend medical credit cards
or loans to Federal health care program beneficiaries (e.g. Medicare, Medicaid, Affordable
Care Act Marketplace, or Children’s Health Insurance Program enrollees)? Is the use of these
products limited to certain types of health care items or services, such as items and services
that are not reimbursable by Medicare or another third-party payor?
Any time there is a high deductible plan being used to purchase an expensive service that is
predictable, you will see the introduction of medical credit cards. Birth. Further, any time you
segregate the market availability of a desirable service by payer, eg Medicaid not paying for
orthodontics, weight reduction surgery or lasik, you will see medical credit cards.

Do medical payment product companies recommend certain health care providers to their
users?
No idea. Ask the Medical Payment companies.



Do companies limit where or how patients use medical credit cards?
No idea.  Ask the Medical Payment companies.

Is the health care provider (or the medical payment product company) offsetting some of the
patient’s medical debt or providing any other incentives to the patient (e.g., travel rewards
for charges to the card)?
I don’t see this.

What policy actions should Treasury consider taking to address problematic practices related
to medical credit cards or loans, including debt collection and credit reporting practices, to
conform with the existing tax laws and regulations pertaining to tax exempt hospitals?
See IRS testimony from Ge Bai, John Hopkins.  The audacity of “non-for-profit” hospitals limiting
access by Medicaid and non-eligible Mothers is well known in the birth community.  The
hospital employs a Provider moat strategy that don’t accept Medicaid, self-pay or
underperforming insurance plans.   The only way in is through an additional ER claim.

Should a tax-exempt hospital’s signing patients up for medical payment products be
considered similar to a tax-exempt hospital’s selling medical debt, such that the special rules
that only exclude debt sales from being extraordinary collection actions if certain
requirements are met would be applied to these payment products?
On what basis should a tax-exempt hospital be selling medical debt when it has an obligation to
provide charity care to retain its IRS status?  See Ge Bai and small Claims Court example.

How would applying the debt sale special rules to payment products change hospitals’ and
payment product providers’ current practices, especially those related to financial assistance
eligibility screening, extraordinary collection actions, interest rates, and recall or return of
balances owed by FAP-eligible individuals?
I have no idea.  Ask the Hospitals.

How do tax-exempt hospitals’ promotion of medical payment products compare to their
operationalization of the requirement that their financial assistance policies be widely
publicized?
Please look into the largest Non-For-Profit IDNs in America, birth policies and drill into the
Provider Moat/EMTALA reality.

What are best practices for hospitals publishing and making patients aware of financial
assistance programs (beyond compliance with the widely publicized requirements found in
the section 501(r) regulations)?
People don’t’ inquire where they aren’t allowed in the first place.  New hospitals are placing
themselves within monitored/gated communities so they aren’t easily found by people who
would need assistance.  Cross reference healthiest counties with new Hospitals.



Are medical payment product companies advertising their products as delivering community
benefits or as a form of financial assistance?
See marketing material.

Are tax-exempt hospitals claiming that their promotion of medical payment products delivers
community benefits or provides financial assistance, including in their filings of Form 990,
Schedule H?
Ask Hospitals.

Does the availability of medical payment products generally benefit the community or assist
patients financially?
They can serve as a financial tool for savvy consumers.  More likely, they are serving as a stop
gap that reflects a failure in health plan design and health policy.


In summary, the advent of medical credit cards in the pregnancy economy reflects a breakdown
in US Federal healthcare policy and opportunity for innovation and care delivery
transformation.  Please invite me and other small business owners like me to the table to
contribute to solutions.   Thank you.

Sincerely,

Kendra Wyatt, Chief Executive Officer
kendra@newbirthcompany.com
New Birth Company LLC
9202 West 110th ST
Overland Park, KS 66210
(m) 816-210-8964

Together, we do more

A funding solution that supports

WELCOME TO UNITED CREDIT
Welcome! We’re excited you’re part of our merchant network.
United Credit is proud to be your partner and support your business growth through a ﬁnancing option that helps
consumers get the services and products they need.
We have relationships with multiple lenders, which means we have a variety of ﬁnancing options to serve a wide variety of
your customers. Our team is here to support you and your business every step of the way so you can focus on what
matters most: your customers.
Ready to get started? This guide will help you through the process.
01

How we help
YOUR BUSINESS

Dedicated support

our customer support team - guides your customers
through the process, taking the burden oﬀ your staﬀ.

02

customers pay for larger expenses.
Strong approvals
We support your ongoing growth with strong
consumer approval rates*.
Quick funding
Once a customer is approved and secures ﬁnancing,
your business receives payment quickly.
Ongoing support
We offer live customer service and account
managers to support your business.
No hidden fees
We never charge enrollment or setup fees. There are
no monthly or annual fees, either - you only pay fees
when a loan is processed.
*United Credit partners with multiple lenders, some of whom offer
2nd look options for consumers with sub-prime credit, which
expands opportunities for approvals. Ask us for more details.

The United Credit advantage
When it comes to ﬁnancing a major or larger purchase, your customers need options.
Here’s how United Credit can help, and how we compare to other ﬁnancing options.
We are not a credit card.
United Credit provides your customer with access to an installment loan, not a credit card. That means we help them get
ﬁnancing for exactly what they need, nothing more. We break those payments into easy-to-understand terms, a set APR,
and monthly payments that are easy to manage.
We can be a stronger option than personal checks or cash.
Some customers may have savings they’d like to use for their purchases. While this is an option, it could mean draining their
personal savings or emergency fund to cover the cost. Using a ﬁnancing option for their purchase keeps that cash on hand
in case it’s needed later. Plus, they can use it to pay oﬀ their loan early without a penalty!
03

Oﬀering ﬁnancing to customers
Want to talk to your customers about their ﬁnancing options but aren’t sure where
to begin? Here are some tips to get the conversation started.
Have a conversation
Tell your customers financing is offered through lending
partners on the United Credit platform. If they have
questions, refer them to our website at unitedcredit.com.
Give them time
Some customers may be ready to make a purchase right
away, and others may need time to consider their options.
Do not pressure them into financing. Oﬀer them patience
and kindness along the way, ensuring they know you want
them to make the best decision for themselves and their
budget.
Share information
Let every customer know your business offers financing.
Even if they aren’t interested in ﬁnancing their purchase in
that moment, they may consider it later when they are
ready to make that major purchase.

attachment_2: MERCHANT TRANSPARENCY GUIDELINES
United Credit promotes full transparency and disclosure to all applicants. To assure that applicants are not
mislead and are aware of key information about United Credit, you hereby agree as follows:
● United Credit works with multiple lenders to offer closed end, unsecured, personal
installment loans to borrowers.
● No person on your staff who has not completed training for the United Credit program will discuss
the program with prospective borrowers.
● You and your staff will not make any implied or overt promises that an applicant will be approved
for a loan through United Credit
● You and your staff will not quote any particular interest rate, term, or other loan feature to a
prospective borrower with United Credit
● You and your staff agree you will print adverse action notices at the request of the prospective
borrower.
● You will direct all potential applicants to apply directly through the United Credit application by
providing them an application link via the merchant portal OR with a United Credit brochure that
includes our website application link.
● You will not suggest/direct patients/clients who are under duress (general sedation, in extreme
discomfort, or heavily medicated) to apply through United Credit
● You will not suggest/direct patients/clients to apply with United Credit if you have reason to believe
they are intending to commit fraud.
● You will direct all patient/client inquiries about the program or their loan to United Credit for
handling or referral to the appropriate lender as applicable.
● You will accept United Credit loan proceeds only for those costs incurred or services rendered within
30 days of receipt of funds. If services are not rendered within 30 days, the borrower has the right to
an automatic refund from you for services not yet rendered.
● You will maintain a fair refund policy and will advise all patients/clients of your policy.
● Comply with all United Credit policies and procedures and all applicable local, state and federal laws
and regulations, including non-discrimination laws and the customer’s right of rescission
● United Credit reserves the right to monitor your adherence to these and other United Credit policies.
Providers who violate these policies will be subject to chargebacks as well as potential termination
from the program

Federal Reserve, X9 ISO20022 Working Group on Faster Healthcare Payments
From:  Kendra Wyatt, New Birth Company LLC, Chief Executive Officer
Re:
Request for Information Regarding Medical Payment Products

I am answering this RFI as a State Licensed Healthcare Provider, who is in-network with Kansas
and Missouri Medicaid, Tricare, Federal Exchange Insurance Products and 3rd Party Commercial
Insurance Plans.  I am reviewing the use of medical payment credit cards for my business right
now due to high deductible maternity benefits and slow insurance plan payments.

The bluntness in my RFI response reflects the intolerable length of time it takes for small
healthcare businesses to be paid by Insurance Plans and TPAs reviewing claims. 60+ days is
common for Federal Exchange and TPA plans. In some cases, we are faxing and mailing claims.
These systems can’t support the 10s of ICD10 codes needed to back up modern Social
Determinant of Health (SODH) risk assessments.

We can’t talk about faster payments in healthcare without talking about the impact of
ISO20022 on the American banking system and that impact on the small business owner. Large
banks and vendors are working on ISO20022 adoption and the ability to bill their customers
(me) in real time.  Large institutions will leverage fast-payment bank products into basis point
discounts and small business providers will be stuck with no banking float.

Mr. Mick Talley and I co-chair an X9 ISO20022 Faster Healthcare Payments Working Group. I
recommend you interview me, Mr. Talley, Mr. Stephen Ranzini with University Bank and Mr.
David Repking at JPMorganChase, Chair of X9C and ISO 20022 Market Practice Industry Forum.
Guy Berg and Carolyn Kroll with the Chicago Federal Reserve are key leaders in the faster
payments space.  I recommend talking to Professor Ge Bai, John Hopkins regarding Hospitals
non-for-profit tax status.

In the spirit of “if you oppose, propose” I ask the Biden Administration, CFPB, CMS, HHS, the
ONC, Treasury and the Federal Reserve to sponsor 4 Corner Pilots of “Faster Payments” in the
healthcare sector.  We already have a (4 Corner) healthcare use case using the Federal Reserve
framework. Let’s learn by doing. We are underutilizing our national HIT infrastructure; and
driving out small business providers in our underserved, rural and tribal communities. Doing
nothing represents the status quo of friction in 18.3% of the GDP. The use of medical credit
cards is a symptom; the disease is friction and lack of innovation.  We must create the low-
friction policy environment where healthcare providers can survive and thrive.  Let’s support
alternate market forces to compete head-to-head with robber barons who make money off the
friction.  Thank you for listening.  I welcome your inquiries regarding my responses.



What are the benefits, costs, and risks of medical payment products for consumers, health
care providers, and companies offering these products?
Our APRN Midwife Led & State Licensed Birth Center provides low-risk pregnant women with
prenatal, birth, and birth facility services for mothers and their newborns.  We represent the
best-case scenario of a low-risk birth and appropriate resourced birth experience.

There is a myth in US healthcare that “Pregnancy & Birth” is covered 100% by all American
insurance plans.  This is simply NOT THE CASE.  High deductibles are being applied to low-risk
birth.

Plan deductibles of $2000, $3000, $4000, and $5000 are commonly applied deductibles for
maternity services.  HHS needs to compare the differences in payments made through
Medicaid, Federal Employee Health Plan and the Federal Exchange Plans.  The Federal Exchange
Plans have the least programming and the highest deductibles for Maternity Services. The cost
of the newborn is paid separately from the mother, adding additional cost.  I have no desire to
take these young families to small claims court, yet my unpaid patient accounts receivables is
currently over $100,000.

What are the terms of medical payment products, including interest rates and fees?
My understanding it varies based on the terms of the loan. I’ve attached marketing material
from one company.

How much debt do consumers carry on medical credit cards and loans in total, and what is
the average individual debt level?  If we move forward with this product, the patient debt will
be from $1000-$5000 dollars reflecting their plan deductible.

How concentrated is the medical payment product market, and what role do private equity
firms play in this market?  I do not know.  I only have exposure to Care Credit and United
Credit.

Are there specific populations (e.g., race, socioeconomic status, gender identity, sexual
orientation, age, language, etc.) or geographic regions that experience disproportionately
higher utilization of medical payment products?  Look at Maternity Care. Look at States where
there is no Medicaid expansion and where the Medicaid income levels for Maternity coverage
are low.

What are the health equity impacts of medical payment products and related billing and
collection policies and practices? Many OB, Pediatric, Dentist and Orthodontic Offices do not
participate in Medicaid.  So, by definition, access to medical credit is only way for these patients
to access highly desirable services where the Provider refuses to accept less than
Medicare/Cost based reimbursement.


It is an American policy failure that we haven’t adopted aligned multi-payer policy and payment
environment for high utilization and highly desirable outcomes.
Align Birth across Medicaid, Federal Exchange, Tricare, VA CCN, Federal Employee Health Plan.
PROTIP:  **Providers will change behavior if you pay them quickly. **

Kansas Medicaid pays the Pregnancy and Birth Global of 59400 70% of Medicare.  Certified
Nurse Midwives are only paid 75% of the OB Global, which is only 50% of Medicare.  The Birth
Center reimbursement of $1295 hasn’t been increased for 20 years.

Do medical payment products affect members of specific underserved communities
differently, including members of Tribal communities and geographically isolated
communities?  I’m thinking many of these places of no access to capital.  Ask Matt Libman, CEO
of United Credit and Beto Casellas, CEO of Care Credit where they offer their product.

Do certain products or policies present opportunities to better serve members of
underserved communities? Yes. Suggestion….US Government sponsors pregnant families with
personal health bank accounts with first $10,000 to purchase maternity/birth care as they see
fit, subject to IRS eligibility criteria, allowing families to bank unused amount in child’s name.
Implement this for all Americans including Medicaid eligible. Any amount over $10,000 subject
to healthcare insurance rules and case management.

Patients can pay for care in many different ways, such as by medical credit card or loan,
general purpose credit card, insurance, or through a zero-interest payment plan. What are
the costs and benefits for health care providers of offering each of these methods? Are there
situations where one method of payment is more advantageous than another?
As a small provider, I have to accept credit cards for these deductables or I wouldn’t be paid.  I
lose 5% to the cost of credit card processing. I have no ability to finance long term payment
plans. Large provider health systems have plenty of capital to support this as an option.  Thus,
our current economic system empowers very large systems over small entrepreneurs and
business owners.
What incentives do financial services companies offer health care providers, including
revenue-sharing or other financial or non-financial incentives? Largest incentive to take this
product is time.   I’m having to pay a fee upfront to recoup 85% of the fee that would typically
take me months to receive; and that is after I bug and nag and send people to collections.  We
are failing people.

The rich have the most free time, the rich never wait in line in person, don’t have to fax paper,
don’t have to “go down to the agency”.  HCA or Kaiser can chase down $1000 when it has
rooms of people to wait people out and lawyers to take people to small claims court.  See
attached list of Non for Profit Hospital Cases at Small Claims Court.



How do medical payment products and health insurance coverage interact? Do group health
plan or health insurance issuer practices contribute to uptake of medical payment products
by patients and providers?  The medical payment product market is a direct result of slow
payments by insurance companies and pathetic plan design for high utilization procedures such
as birth. Instead of innovating more efficient care delivery models and faster payments to
incent quality; your plans are moving costs to higher deductibles.

How many days do providers typically have to wait to be paid by plans or issuers versus by
medical payment product companies or general purpose credit card companies? My
credit/debit processor settles within 48 hours.  The fastest insurance plan can pay a claim in
two weeks for a perfect claim.  The TPAs and Federal Exchange Plans can easily take up to 90
days and that is based on us chasing them down repeatedly.  The TPA plans have the highest
deductibles, thus the medical credit market was born.  ISO20022 predicts I’m paid in minutes.

What factors, such as administrative requirements or clinical reviews, contribute to any
differential resolution timelines?  Yes- because CMS requires fee for service claims as a
requirement of participation. These claims are rekeyed by hand by person and people make
many mistakes. We should be paid off of our clinical data in our EHRs.

Please direct HHS, CMS, Office of the National Coordinator and Federal Reserve to pilot 4
Corner payment models in 2023.  We’ve had the ONC and federally funded EHRs for 13 years.
Why aren’t Plans paying on clinical data instead of claims starting with the most simple of
pediatric and maternal/women’s health visits and birth?   You start at the beginning with the
highly desired, predictable easy transactions. The best security against fraudulent payments is
rapid payments incenting the provider to give up 100% transparent clinical data.

Does a patient’s use of a medical payment product exempt them from certain consumer
protections, provider requirements, or group health plan or health insurance issuer
requirements? No. Not that I’m aware of.

Are different types of health coverage treated differently?
Yes.  Let’s consider the history of racial segregation on American pregnancy and birth. We are in
a multi-payer Safety Net/FQHC/Academic Medical Center vs the Private Suburban and Rural
markets fragmented reality.    As a result, we don’t have the Adam Smith economic market
driving equity and outcomes.  We need the Federal Government to align agencies towards an
outcome. Not all consumers have equal access to all Providers.

Does health care provider organizational structure, including ownership by private equity,
affect providers’ decisions to offer and promote these products?
Sure.  Yes. Does this factor compare to taking or not taking low Medicaid payments ??  We
wouldn’t exist if I were a non-for-profit.  Funding my own business was the only way to raise
capital.  I take both states Medicaid.


What are some best practices for health care providers who offer medical payment products
in avoiding adverse financial and health impacts for patients? i. Are there specific tactics or
practices that are well tailored and adapted for use by health care professionals in and
serving underserved communities, including Tribal communities and geographically isolated
communities?
See great idea of funding first $10,000 for every pregnancy/child. Empower the people not the
institutions.

What actions should the agencies take to develop and encourage uptake of these established
best practices? iii. Are there examples of actions or best practices at the State or local level to
which the Federal government should look?
Google “Child to College” and “Baby Bonds”.  There are many people across the political
spectrum that would be likely to support the first $10,000 idea.  Invest in American family’s
financial literacy during pregnancy and birth.  Set them up for success.

To what extent are patients using medical payment products to pay bills that are incorrect, or
that could be covered or defrayed by lower-cost alternatives?
Better plan design for Birth; eg cover 100% and these products won’t have a market in
maternity care.  Start there.   There is so much infertility due to untreated diabetes.  Let’s get
healthcare for women right first.

What billing errors may patients commonly encounter?
Patient not eligible.  Not aware less than 26 years old are on their parent or step parent plan.
Baby not included on Parent/Grandparent Plan.  Plan deems Provider not eligible or not in-
network.  Need one single CAQH like system.  The idea that you must credential your six
providers with every plan and TPA is a terrible Provider burden and waste of everyone’s time.

How does using a medical payment product affect patients’ rights to dispute incorrect bills?
It shouldn’t.  That isn’t in our best interest.  Many providers game the system as an “out of
network” provider.  We don’t. Using the medical payment plan is last resort assuming Mom is in
good standing.  Again, we are talking about poor plan design with $4000 deductibles. New
parents know the least about navigating insurance and have the least time to figure it out.

Are certain groups of patients, such as members of specific underserved communities, more
likely to experience medical billing errors or issues resolving disputes over bills paid using
medical payment products? It is our experience that the most vulnerable working Americans,
whether in the urban core or in rural communities have the most poorly designed benefits.  If
they work for the school, they have high deductibles. If they work at a small business, they have
high deductibles.   (NOTE- look at Nashville Public School and Vanderbilt for example of doing it
right).  The inequity is that only the richest most affluent people working at corporate offices
have the best benefits.  The poorest have access to Medicaid but equal access to all Providers.
Ineligible Moms in labor are lucky if they are served when they walk into the ER under EMTALA.


What actions should agencies consider taking to better understand the effects of medical
payment products on consumers and the health care industry, educate consumers and
providers about the risks of these products, and collect complaints?
Send my RFI comments around.  You should start with fixing Birth.  This financial situation is
depressing the birth rate.  There is a bureaucratic fear of addressing pregnancy head on due to
abortion. Get healthy birth right. Increase likelihood good things can happen.

What are some sources of data on medical payment products? What additional data are
needed to understand the impact of medical payment products on patients and the health
care industry?  Backward engineer Federal Exchange Plan claims with high deductibles.
Analyze those CPTs. Ask direct questions. Ask the credit companies to give testimony.

What data collection, data analysis, and research actions should agencies take? iii. Are there
different or other actions that agencies should consider for underserved communities,
including Tribal communities and geographically isolated communities?
Identify and pay Community Health Workers in underserved, rural and Tribal communities to
ask around.  People close to the trenches know the answers.  Have you asked your FQHC
partners how people are making it work?

What types of consumer complaints have States and localities received?
No idea.  My complaint is that we OK every drug that comes on the market, but we can’t pay for
a healthy birth that would make the drug not necessary in the future.  Our values are screwed
up.

Where medical payment products are causing harm, what are some specific levers for
regulatory oversight and enforcement by Federal agencies that regulate financial products or
health care providers?
We are talking about outpatient procedures vs inpatient procedures right ?  These products are
sold as loans vs credit cards…is a bank involved?  Who holds the funds for the Credit
companies?  What regulatory oversight do you have over a clinic when the Plan isn’t Medicare?
You don’t have that oversight at the federal level.  You should be pushing best practice out
through the Federal Exchange Plans and Federal Employee Health Plan to impact BCBS, United
and Centene.  You must partner with States and Boards of Nursing and Boards of Healing Arts
to solicit and educate on complaints.  You can also check with malpractice companies.

Are there specific areas for Federal enforcement actions? Are there examples of regulation or
enforcement at the State or local level to which the Federal government should look?
Go fix plan design first.  Skipping to law enforcement when we haven’t fixed the main problem
is half our SDOH problems in this country. Focus on removing demand from the market.





What complementary legislative actions are worth exploring? Where may additional
statutory authority be needed?
Identify the difference between the necessary use of these products that reflect policy failure
and poor plan design (Birth) vs funding of Dog Hip Replacement (discretionary income).  And
then use that data to inform legislative action.

I have not used this type of product myself. I’m not going to comment on this section
regarding Individuals.

What actions should the CFPB consider taking to address problematic practices related to
medical credit cards or loans, including debt collection and credit reporting practices ?
Assess the reasons why the cards are being used the first place.  See above.

How do firms offering medical financial products typically market to providers?
We are cold-called/solicited through our licenses (public/purchasable), trade associations and
membership organizations.

How do creditors and their affiliates underwrite loans to patients? What specific factors (e.g.,
age, type of medical procedure, credit score, etc.) are considered in underwriting?
I don’t know.  They seem to be happy to support “Birth” given the large deductibles described.
Eg, “they have 75 providers in this space”.

Do consumers understand the risks of paying medical bills via a medical credit card,
installment loan, or other commercial payment product, including lowered ability to
negotiate their bill with their provider?
If their choices are being take to small claims court over their 20% co-pay and $3000 deductible
or using a card; they are making a rational choice to open an account and pay the bill.  Do you
see how close we are to debtor’s prisons? Garnishing wages for a healthcare bill?

To what extent are alleged debts placed on medical credit cards and loans sent to debt
collectors? How do medical payment product companies’ debt collection practices differ from
those of health care providers, and are any issuer or provider debt collection practices posing
risks to consumers?
I have no idea how they differ.  Being threatened with court is scary.  People calling you day and
night and calling your relatives is scary.  It puts you in a constant state of stress, depression, and
leads to cardiac conditions.  It contributes to substance abuse.  See “these people are the ones
who can least afford these large deductibles”.

How can the CFPB use its authorities to ensure people with medical bills in collections,
including medical payment product debt, are screened for eligibility for financial assistance
and other benefits?
You need a healthcare stabilization fund and availability fund as the “back up” for the indigent
not yet eligible for Medicare, Medicaid and Disability SSI and SSID.   Google “healthcare

stabilization fund” for examples of how states created funds as a last resort for provider
malpractice.

How are health care providers and financial companies using credit or “propensity to pay”
scores to determine patients’ eligibility for financial assistance or medical payment products?
I’m looking at their deductible.  If it’s over $1000 and there isn’t proof of payment plan/income,
then they would meet criteria to receive information on the product.

What are the implications for compliance with the Fair Credit Reporting Act or other CFPB
authorities?
No idea.

When hospitals write off a patient’s debt as uncollectible or “bad debt” and cease attempts
to collect, do they notify patients that collection attempts will cease?
I don’t know.  Ask Hospitals.  Ask Ambulatory Surgery Centers. We provide a final letter that the
person is in good standing if they make good faith attempts with us to pay their bill.  We are on
a cash basis. Look at IRS policy how to lift up small healthcare providers with annual revenue
less than 5 Million per year who are in-network with Medicaid.

Would patients benefit from such notifications, and would such notifications reduce hospital
revenue? See Ge Bai at John Hopkins. Jumbo non-for-profit hospitals suing its own patients.
Where’s the charity care?  https://www.healthaffairs.org/doi/abs/10.1377/hlthaff.2020.01627

What actions should HHS consider taking to address problematic practices related to medical
credit cards or loans, particularly as they relate to patients eligible for or enrolled in
Medicare, Medicaid, or the Children’s Health Insurance Program, or patients enrolled in
Affordable Care Act Marketplace plans?
Start with the ACA Marketplace high deductible plans and paying for pregnancy and birth.
This is a policy failure.

What types of health insurance (Medicare, Medicaid, private insurance, etc.) are particularly
associated with the likelihood that an individual is offered or makes use of medical credit
cards and loans, and how does the type of health coverage affect relevant provider billing
practices?
ACA Marketplace plans followed by Self Insured Employers with Third Party Administrators are
the WORST offenders creating the poor plan design circumstances where these products are
needed.  Overlay high-deductible plans with low poverty requirements (must be very poor) to
obtain Medicaid.  Here are your problem areas.

Are there particular health care provider types that are most associated with being offered or
offering medical payment products, and are these providers receiving directed payments or
other incentives through State Medicaid programs?
Certified Nurse Midwives and Birth Centers are not being solicited.


Has the No Surprises Act and its surprise billing protections affected the prevalence and use
of medical credit cards and loans, and if so, how? i. Has the notice and consent process been
used to promote medical cards and loans to patients seeking health care items or services
from out-of-network providers/facilities, and if so, how?
We are just seeing the beginning of transparency for birth services come through on the
Hospital Web Sites.  There is still much confusion as the Hospital isn’t clear on what is a Facility
Charge vs the Professional Charge, Anesthesia, Facility for Mom, Facility Charge for Baby,
Pediatrician bill.  Please come down on the Hospitals and make this crystal clear for a basic
NVSD and a normal newborn. These are the #1 utilized hospital admissions and we still don’t
have basic consumer transparency.  There are still many opportunities for “surprises”.

For example, in instances where the No Surprises Act permits providers and facilities to seek
notice and obtain consent from an insured patient to waive their balance billing and cost-
sharing protections under the No Surprises Act, are providers and facilities impermissibly
attaching or incorporating medical card or loan documents or information to the notice and
consent forms, or giving them to the patient at the same time as the notice and consent
forms?
I can’t speak to this. Talk to hospitals.

What steps are health care providers and facilities putting into place to ensure that bills paid
through medical payment products do not violate surprise billing requirements and that
patients who use medical payment products retain their No Surprises Act rights?
I can’t speak to this.  Talk to hospitals.
How does or might the use of medical credit cards and loans affect the amount and timing of
cost sharing a patient covered through Medicare, Medicaid, and/or the Affordable Care Act
Marketplace owes for a covered service?
It shouldn’t affect either.  The allowable should be the same.

Are there any observable differences in cost sharing among patients belonging to
underserved communities, such as Tribal communities or geographically isolated
communities?
Underserved and rural community members covered by ACA or Employer sponsored TPA plans
tend to have higher co-insurance percentages and higher deductibles.  It isn’t fair at all.

Hospital Price Transparency: What prices or versions of standard charges (e.g., cash prices)
are offered to patients who sign up for a medical credit card or installment loan?
I don’t know.  Ask the Hospitals.

What steps are taken by health care providers to ensure these charges are adequately
disclosed in accordance with hospital price transparency requirements? Do these charges
reflect and specifically identify facility fees?
We are just seeing the beginning of transparency for birth services come through on the
Hospital Web Sites.  There is still much confusion as the Hospital isn’t clear on what is a Facility
Charge vs the Professional Charge, Anesthesia, Facility for Mom, Facility Charge for Baby,

Pediatrician. Please come down on the Hospitals and make the total cost for a basic NVSD and a
normal newborn. These are the #1 utilized hospital admissions and we still don’t have basic
consumer transparency.

How might HHS improve patient understanding of options for covering the cost of medical
treatments? At what points in the care process could patients be provided with information
about their financial obligations and payment options?
We should have the ability to purchase a birth bundle and see true apples to apples on quality
with CNMs and Birth Centers. For the 1000th time, the family healthcare literacy journey starts
when a woman is pregnant and gives birth. It’s the first time we use our insurance. You educate
her and her partner on price transparency; you are educating her family for the lifespan.   She is
family medical case manager 80% of the time.  Dear Treasury and Federal Reserve- invest in
Mom/Partner Baby Financial Literacy as part of Pregnancy Childbirth Classes.  Let’s go pilot
these together and invest in American families.

What financial relationships exist between medical payment product companies and health
care providers? For example, do companies provide financial incentives to providers who
enroll patients in medical payment products?
None to my knowledge.  It’s a service sold for a fee.

Do providers pay financial companies to collect patients’ overdue balances? Or, do providers
have arrangements with financial companies to indemnify the company in whole or in part if
the patient defaults, such as an arrangement that when patients default on their debt to the
financial company, the debt reverts to the provider?
Yes, providers pay their billing companies to collect balances every day as part of the 5.75% we
are charged to process claims on top of the 5% we lose to credit card processing fees for paid
deductibles.  Some providers “sell” their debt to collection agencies.  Ask the big guys who do
that- look them up by NAIC codes.

Do health care providers or financial institutions market or recommend medical credit cards
or loans to Federal health care program beneficiaries (e.g. Medicare, Medicaid, Affordable
Care Act Marketplace, or Children’s Health Insurance Program enrollees)? Is the use of these
products limited to certain types of health care items or services, such as items and services
that are not reimbursable by Medicare or another third-party payor?
Any time there is a high deductible plan being used to purchase an expensive service that is
predictable, you will see the introduction of medical credit cards. Birth. Further, any time you
segregate the market availability of a desirable service by payer, eg Medicaid not paying for
orthodontics, weight reduction surgery or lasik, you will see medical credit cards.

Do medical payment product companies recommend certain health care providers to their
users?
No idea. Ask the Medical Payment companies.



Do companies limit where or how patients use medical credit cards?
No idea.  Ask the Medical Payment companies.

Is the health care provider (or the medical payment product company) offsetting some of the
patient’s medical debt or providing any other incentives to the patient (e.g., travel rewards
for charges to the card)?
I don’t see this.

What policy actions should Treasury consider taking to address problematic practices related
to medical credit cards or loans, including debt collection and credit reporting practices, to
conform with the existing tax laws and regulations pertaining to tax exempt hospitals?
See IRS testimony from Ge Bai, John Hopkins.  The audacity of “non-for-profit” hospitals limiting
access by Medicaid and non-eligible Mothers is well known in the birth community.  The
hospital employs a Provider moat strategy that don’t accept Medicaid, self-pay or
underperforming insurance plans.   The only way in is through an additional ER claim.

Should a tax-exempt hospital’s signing patients up for medical payment products be
considered similar to a tax-exempt hospital’s selling medical debt, such that the special rules
that only exclude debt sales from being extraordinary collection actions if certain
requirements are met would be applied to these payment products?
On what basis should a tax-exempt hospital be selling medical debt when it has an obligation to
provide charity care to retain its IRS status?  See Ge Bai and small Claims Court example.

How would applying the debt sale special rules to payment products change hospitals’ and
payment product providers’ current practices, especially those related to financial assistance
eligibility screening, extraordinary collection actions, interest rates, and recall or return of
balances owed by FAP-eligible individuals?
I have no idea.  Ask the Hospitals.

How do tax-exempt hospitals’ promotion of medical payment products compare to their
operationalization of the requirement that their financial assistance policies be widely
publicized?
Please look into the largest Non-For-Profit IDNs in America, birth policies and drill into the
Provider Moat/EMTALA reality.

What are best practices for hospitals publishing and making patients aware of financial
assistance programs (beyond compliance with the widely publicized requirements found in
the section 501(r) regulations)?
People don’t’ inquire where they aren’t allowed in the first place.  New hospitals are placing
themselves within monitored/gated communities so they aren’t easily found by people who
would need assistance.  Cross reference healthiest counties with new Hospitals.



Are medical payment product companies advertising their products as delivering community
benefits or as a form of financial assistance?
See marketing material.

Are tax-exempt hospitals claiming that their promotion of medical payment products delivers
community benefits or provides financial assistance, including in their filings of Form 990,
Schedule H?
Ask Hospitals.

Does the availability of medical payment products generally benefit the community or assist
patients financially?
They can serve as a financial tool for savvy consumers.  More likely, they are serving as a stop
gap that reflects a failure in health plan design and health policy.


In summary, the advent of medical credit cards in the pregnancy economy reflects a breakdown
in US Federal healthcare policy and opportunity for innovation and care delivery
transformation.  Please invite me and other small business owners like me to the table to
contribute to solutions.   Thank you.

Sincerely,

Kendra Wyatt, Chief Executive Officer
kendra@newbirthcompany.com
New Birth Company LLC
9202 West 110th ST
Overland Park, KS 66210
(m) 816-210-8964
